title: Computer Vision for Robots Fundamentals
objective: Understand how robots extract useful information from camera images, from basic image processing to deep learning-based object detection, and how vision integrates with the broader robot perception stack.
beginnerExplanation:
A camera captures an image as a grid of pixels, each with colour values. To a computer, an image is just a large array of numbers — it has no inherent understanding of what those numbers mean. Computer vision is the field that bridges this gap, extracting meaningful information from raw pixel data.

The simplest vision operations process individual pixels. Thresholding converts a colour image to black and white based on brightness — useful for separating a bright object from a dark background. Colour filtering isolates pixels of a specific colour — a robot in a warehouse might look for red bins. Blurring smooths out noise. Edge detection finds boundaries between regions, highlighting the outlines of objects.

Feature detection goes further. Algorithms like SIFT and ORB find distinctive points in an image — corners, blobs, edges — that can be recognised even if the camera moves or the lighting changes. By matching features between two images taken from different positions, you can estimate how the camera moved (visual odometry) or reconstruct the 3D structure of the scene.

The revolution in robot vision came with deep learning. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on millions of labelled images can now detect and classify objects with superhuman accuracy. Models like YOLO (You Only Look Once) can identify dozens of objects in a single image in real time — fast enough for a robot picking items from a conveyor belt. In Australian agriculture, CNNs trained on images of crops can distinguish weeds from valuable plants, enabling precision spraying robots that reduce herbicide use by up to 90%.
deeperExplanation:
For robotics applications, computer vision extends far beyond simple 2D image processing. Robots need to understand the 3D world — not just what objects are in the scene, but where they are in physical space.

Camera calibration is the essential first step. Every camera has intrinsic parameters (focal length, principal point, distortion coefficients) that define how 3D world points project onto the 2D image plane. Calibrating the camera — typically by imaging a known pattern like a checkerboard — determines these parameters, enabling accurate geometric reasoning from image data.

Stereo vision uses two cameras separated by a known baseline to compute depth. By finding the same point in both images (stereo matching), the disparity (pixel offset) between the two views can be converted to distance using triangulation. This mimics human depth perception. Structured light sensors (like the Intel RealSense D435) project a known pattern and observe its deformation to compute dense depth maps. Time-of-flight cameras measure the round-trip time of light pulses for each pixel.

Object detection using deep learning has transformed robotic manipulation. YOLO (You Only Look Once) and its variants can detect and localise multiple objects in real time (30+ frames per second). For robotic grasping, instance segmentation models like Mask R-CNN go further, providing pixel-perfect outlines of each object — essential for planning grasp points on irregularly shaped items.

Point clouds — 3D representations where each point has x, y, z coordinates (and optionally colour) — are the bridge between vision and physical robot action. Whether generated from stereo cameras, structured light, or LiDAR, point clouds represent the robot's 3D understanding of the scene. Algorithms for point cloud processing include plane fitting (finding surfaces), clustering (separating objects), registration (aligning multiple scans), and pose estimation (determining an object's position and orientation in 6-DOF).

In Australian field conditions, vision systems face particular challenges. Harsh sunlight creates extreme contrast and glare. Dust coats camera lenses. Rain droplets scatter light. Temperature changes cause thermal expansion that can shift calibration. Robust robot vision systems account for these environmental factors through hardware (lens hoods, wipers, environmental housings) and software (adaptive exposure, white balance, real-time recalibration).
keyTakeaways: Computer vision extracts meaning from raw pixel data through processing, feature detection, and deep learning
Camera calibration is essential for accurate geometric reasoning from images
Stereo vision and depth cameras provide 3D understanding of the scene
Deep learning models like YOLO enable real-time object detection for robotic applications
Point clouds bridge 2D vision with 3D robotic action
Environmental conditions (sun, dust, rain) significantly affect vision system performance in the field
example: At a major Australian e-commerce fulfilment centre, a robotic picking system uses computer vision to identify and grasp items from jumbled bins. An Intel RealSense depth camera mounted on the robot arm captures both a colour image and a depth map of the bin contents. A YOLO-based deep learning model, trained on images of the warehouse's 50,000+ SKUs, identifies each visible item and its boundaries in the colour image. The depth map is then used to determine the 3D position and surface normal of each item. The system selects the item that is most accessible (on top, unobstructed), computes a grasp point and approach angle, and commands the robot arm to pick it. The entire vision pipeline — from raw image to grasp command — runs in under 200 milliseconds, enabling the robot to pick over 1,000 items per hour.
commonMistakes: Training a vision model in the lab with controlled lighting and expecting it to work in the field — domain adaptation is essential
Ignoring camera calibration — uncalibrated cameras produce geometrically incorrect measurements
Using 2D bounding boxes when the robot needs 3D position — depth information is essential for physical interaction
Forgetting that vision processing takes time — latency in the perception pipeline affects control performance
Not collecting enough diverse training data — deep learning models fail on scenarios they have not seen
exercise: Install OpenCV (pip install opencv-python) and write a Python script that: (1) loads an image from file, (2) converts it to grayscale, (3) applies Canny edge detection, (4) finds contours, and (5) draws bounding boxes around detected objects. Test it on an image of several objects on a table. Then extend the script to filter by colour — isolate only red objects by converting to HSV colour space and applying a colour threshold. This exercise introduces the basic OpenCV pipeline that underpins much of robotic vision.

quiz.question: What does YOLO stand for in the context of object detection, and what makes it significant for robotics?
options: Yet Only Larger Objects — it only detects large objects
You Only Look Once — it detects multiple objects in a single pass through the network, enabling real-time performance
Yield Optimised Linear Output — it produces linear bounding box coordinates
Young Object Learning Objective — it learns from few examples
quiz.explanation: YOLO (You Only Look Once) processes the entire image in a single forward pass through the neural network, outputting bounding boxes and class probabilities for all detected objects simultaneously. This single-pass approach is much faster than earlier methods that scanned the image with sliding windows, making YOLO fast enough for real-time robotic applications at 30+ frames per second.

quiz.question: Why is camera calibration important for robotic vision?
options: It makes the camera take sharper photos
It increases the camera's frame rate
It determines the camera's intrinsic parameters, enabling accurate geometric measurements from images
It reduces the file size of captured images
quiz.explanation: Camera calibration determines intrinsic parameters (focal length, principal point, lens distortion) that define how 3D world points project onto the 2D image. Without calibration, you cannot accurately convert pixel coordinates to real-world measurements — a robot would not know the true position or size of objects it sees.

perspectives.visionEngineer: As a vision engineer, you design and train perception models, optimise inference speed for real-time deployment on embedded hardware, and handle the gap between lab performance and field performance.
perspectives.applicationEngineer: As an application engineer, you integrate vision systems into robotic workflows — mounting cameras, configuring lighting, training models on customer-specific objects, and tuning the pipeline for the required throughput and accuracy.
perspectives.dataScientist: As a data scientist supporting a vision system, you manage training datasets, design data collection campaigns, handle annotation workflows, and monitor model performance over time as product catalogues or environments change.
